parts.label: (a)
parts.answer: f(1) = 1 + 1 + 2 + a + b = 7 or 4 + a + b = 7 $\implies$ a + b = 3

markAllocation.criterion: f(1) = 1 + 1 + 2 + a + b = 7 or 4 + a + b = 7 $\implies$ a + b = 3 (as required) AG cso

parts.guidance: M1: For attempting either f(1) or f( 1). A1: For applying f(1) , setting the result equal to 7, and manipulating this correctly to give the result given on the paper as $a+b=3$. Note that the answer is given in part (a). Alternative M1: For long division by $(x-1)$ to give a remainder in a and b which is independent of x. A1: Or Remainder $4+a+b=7$ leading to the correct result of $a+b=3$ (answer given).

parts.label: (b)

markAllocation.criterion: Solving both equations simultaneously to get as far as a =… or b =…
Any one of a = 9 or b = −6

parts.guidance: M1: Attempting either f( 2) or f(2). A1: correct underlined equation in a and b; e.g. $16-8+8-2a+b=-8$ or equivalent, e.g. $-2a+b=-24$. dM1: An attempt to eliminate one variable from 2 linear simultaneous equations in a and b. Note that this mark is dependent upon the award of the first method mark. A1: Any one of $a=9$ or $b=-6$. A1: Both $a=9$ and $b=-6$ and a correct solution only. Alternative M1: For long division by $(x+2)$ to give a remainder in a and b which is independent of x. A1: For Remainder $b-2(a-8)=-8 \implies -2a+b=-24$. Then dM1A1A1 are applied in the same way as before.

markAllocation.criterion: Use of a correct $S_n$ formula with n = 12 (condone missing brackets around $\frac{7}{8}$)

parts.label: (c)

markAllocation.criterion: Applies $S_N$ (GP only) with $a=20$, $r=\frac{7}{8}$ and “uses” 0.5 and their $S_\infty$ at any point in their working.

Uses the law of logarithms to obtain an equation or an inequality of the form $\log(\frac{7}{8})^N > \log(\frac{0.5}{\text{their } S_\infty})$ or $N > \frac{\log(0.5/\text{their } S_\infty)}{\log(0.875)}$

parts.guidance: An incorrect inequality statement at any stage in a candidate’s working loses the final mark. Some candidates do not realise that the direction of the inequality is reversed in the final line of their solution. BUT it is possible to gain full marks for using =, as long as no incorrect working seen. Alternative: Trial & Improvement Method in (c): M1: Attempts $S_N < 160 - 0.5$ or $S_N$ with at least one value for N > 40 dM1: Attempts $S_N < 160 - 0.5$ or $S_N$ with N = 43 or N = 44 M1: For evidence of examining $160 - S_N$ or $S_N$ for both $N=43$ and $N=44$ with both values correct to 2 DP Eg: $160 - S_{43}$ awrt 0.51 and $160 - S_{44}$ awrt 0.45 or $S_{43}$ awrt 159.49 and $S_{44}$ awrt 159.55 A1 cso: $N=44$ Answer of N = 44 only with no working scores no marks

parts.label: (a)
parts.guidance: B1: For 1.494 B1: For 1.741 (1.740 is B0). Wrong accuracy e.g. 1.49, 1.74 is B1B0

parts.label: (b)
parts.guidance: B1: Need $\frac{1}{2}$ of 0.25 or 0.125 o.e. M1: Requires first bracket to contain first plus last values and second bracket to include no additional values from the three in the table. If the only mistake is to omit one value from second bracket this may be regarded as a slip and M mark can be allowed ( An extra repeated term forfeits the M mark however) x values: M0 if values used in brackets are x values instead of y values A1ft: Follows their answers to part (a) and is for {correct expression} A1: Accept 1.4965, 1.497, or 1.50 only after correct work. (No follow through except one special case below following 1.740 in table). Separate trapezia may be used: B1 for 0.125, M1 for $\frac{1}{2}h(a+b)$ used 3 or 4 times (and A1ft if it is all correct) e.g. $0.125(1+1.251) + 0.125(1.251+1.494) + 0.125(1.741+2)$ is M1 A0 equivalent to missing one term in { } in main scheme.

parts.label: (c)
parts.answer: Decrease the width of the strips / Use more trapezia / Increase the number of strips
parts.guidance: Do not accept use more decimal places

questions.answer: Proof that $n^2+2$ cannot be divisible by 4 for all $n$.

markAllocation.criterion: When n is odd, $n^2$ is odd (odd $\times$ odd = odd) so $n^2+2$ is also odd
So for all odd numbers ,n $n^2+2$ is also odd and so cannot be divisible by 4 (as all numbers in the 4 times table are even)
When n is even, $n^2$ is even and a multiple of 4, so $n^2+2$ cannot be a multiple of 4
Fully correct and exhaustive proof. Award for both of the cases above plus a final statement ''So for all n, $n^2+2$ cannot be divisible by 4''

questions.guidance: Alternative - (algebraic) proof M1: If n is even, $n=2k$, so $n^2+2 = (2k)^2+2 = 4k^2+2 = 4(k^2)+2$ M1: If n is odd, $n=2k+1$, so $n^2+2 = (2k+1)^2+2 = 4k^2+4k+1+2 = 4k^2+4k+3 = 4(k^2+k)+3$ A1: For a partial explanation stating that either of $4(k^2)+2$ or $4(k^2+k)+3$ are not a whole numbers. with some valid reason stating why this means that $n^2+2$ is not a multiple of 4. A1*: Full proof with no errors or omissions. This must include The conjecture Correct notation and algebra for both even and odd numbers A full explanation stating why, for all n, $n^2+2$ is not divisible by 4

parts.label: (a)
parts.guidance: B1: requires at least 3 terms, must include first and last terms, an adjacent term and dots! M1: for reversing series (dots needed) dM1: for adding, must have 2S and be a genuine attempt. Either line is sufficient. Dependent on 1st M1. (Allow first 3 marks for use of l for last term but as given for final mark )

markAllocation.criterion: Use of 600 with a correct formula in an attempt to find N.

parts.label: (c)

markAllocation.criterion: Use of correct sum formula with their integer n = N or N – 1 from part (b) where 3 < N < 52 and a = 200 and d = 20.
$600 \times k$ where k is an integer and 3 < k < 52
A correct un-simplified follow through expression with their k consistent with n so that n + k = 52

parts.guidance: There are no marks in (c) for just finding S52

parts.label: (i)
parts.answer: $x = \frac{4}{11}$ or exact recurring decimal 0.36 after correct work

markAllocation.criterion: Applying the subtraction or addition law of logarithms correctly to make two log terms into one log term.
For RHS of either $2^3$, $2^{-3}$, $2^4$ or $\log_2 (\frac{1}{8})$, $\log_2 8$ or $\log_2 16$ i.e. using connection between log base 2 and 2 to a power.
Obtains correct linear equation in x. usually the one in the scheme and attempts x =

parts.guidance: M1: Applying the subtraction or addition law of logarithms correctly to make two log terms into one log term. M1: For RHS of either $2^3$, $2^{-3}$, $2^4$ or $\log_2 (\frac{1}{8})$, $\log_2 8$ or $\log_2 16$ i.e. using connection between log base 2 and 2 to a power. This may follow an error. Use of $3^2$ is M0 dM1: Obtains correct linear equation in x. usually the one in the scheme and attempts x = A1: cso. Answer of 4/11 with no suspect log work preceding this. Alternative M1: $\log_2 (2x) + 3 = \log_2 (5x+4)$ so $\log_2 (2x) + \log_2 (8) = \log_2 (5x+4)$ earns 2nd M1 (3 replaced by $\log_2 8$) 1st M1: Then $\log_2 (16x) = \log_2 (5x+4)$ earns 1st M1 (addition law of logs) Then final dM1 A1 as before

parts.label: (ii)

markAllocation.criterion: Applies power law of logarithms to replace $3\log_a 2$ by $\log_a 2^3$ or $\log_a 8$
Uses addition law of logs to give $\log_a (2^3/5)$ or $\log_a (8/5)$

parts.guidance: M1: Applies power law of logarithms to replace $3\log_a 2$ by $\log_a 2^3$ or $\log_a 8$ dM1: (Should not be following M0) Uses addition law of logs to give $\log_a (2^3/5)$ or $\log_a (8/5)$

parts.label: (a)
parts.guidance: M1: Obtains $(x \pm 10)^2$ and $(y \pm 8)^2$ May be implied by one correct coordinate A1: (10, 8) Answer only scores both marks. Alternative: Method 2: From $x^2+y^2+2gx+2fy+c=0$ centre is $(-g, -f)$ M1: Obtains $(-10, -8)$ A1: Centre is $(-g, -f)$, and so centre is (10, 8).

parts.label: (b)
parts.guidance: M1: For a correct method leading to $r = \dots$ or $r^2$ Allow "100" + "64" - 139 or an attempt at using $(x-10)^2+(y-8)^2=r^2$ form to identify r= A1*: r = 5 This is a printed answer, so a correct method must be seen. Alternative: (b) M1: Attempts to use $g^2+f^2-c$ or $(r^2=)"100"+"64"-139$ A1*: r = 5 following a correct method.

parts.label: (c)

markAllocation.criterion: Substitute $x = 13$ into the equation of circle and solve quadratic to give y =

parts.guidance: M1: Substitutes $x = 13$ into either form of the circle equation, forms and solves the quadratic equation in y A1: Either y = 4 or 12 A1: Both y = 4 and 12 N.B. This can be attempted via a 3, 4, 5 triangle so spotting this and achieving one value for y is M1 A1. Both values scores M1 A1 A1

parts.label: (d)
parts.guidance: M1: Uses Pythagoras' Theorem to find length OC using their (10,8) M1: Uses Pythagoras' Theorem to find OX. Look for $\sqrt{OC^2-r^2}$ A1: $\sqrt{139}$ only

parts.label: (a)
parts.answer: Substitutes $x=1$ in C1: $y = 10(1) - (1)^2 - 8 = 10 - 1 - 8 = 1$ and in C2: $y = (1)^3 = 1 \implies (1, 1)$ lies on both curves.

markAllocation.criterion: Substitutes $x=1$ in C1: $y = 10x - x^2 - 8 = 10 - 1 - 8 = 1$ and in C2: $y = x^3 = 1^3 = 1 \implies (1, 1)$ lies on both curves.

parts.guidance: B1: Substitutes $x=1$ into both $y=10x-x^2-8$ and $y=x^3$ AND achieves y =1 in both.

parts.label: (b)

markAllocation.criterion: Divides by $(x -1)$ to form a quadratic factor.
For factorising of their quadratic factor.

parts.guidance: B1: Sets equations equal to each other and proceeds to $x^3 + x^2 - 10x + 8 = 0$ M1: Divides by $(x -1)$ to form a quadratic factor. Allow any suitable algebraic method including division or inspection. A1: Correct quadratic factor $(x^2 + 2x - 8)$ M1: For factorising of their quadratic factor. A1: Achieves $x=2$ A1: Coordinates of B = (2, 8)

parts.label: (c)

markAllocation.criterion: For knowing that the area of R = $\int_1^2 (10x - x^2 - 8 - x^3) dx$
For raising the power of x seen in at least three terms.
For using the limits ''2'' and 1 in their integrated expression.

parts.guidance: M1: For knowing that the area of R = $\int_1^2 (10x - x^2 - 8 - x^3) dx$ This may also be scored for finding separate areas and subtracting. M1: For raising the power of x seen in at least three terms. A1: Correct integration. It may be left un-simplified. That is allow $\frac{10}{2}x^2$ for $5x^2$ M1: For using the limits ''2'' and 1 in their integrated expression. If separate areas have been attempted, ''2'' and 1 must be used in both integrated expressions. A1: For $\frac{11}{12}$ or exact equivalent.

parts.label: (i)

markAllocation.criterion: Adds $\pi$ or $2\pi$ to previous value of angle (to give $\frac{4\pi}{3}$ or $\frac{7\pi}{3}$)

parts.guidance: M1: Obtains $\frac{\pi}{3}$. Allow $x=\frac{\pi}{3}$ or even $3\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}$. Need not see working here. May be implied by $\theta=\frac{\pi}{9}$ in final answer (allow $3\theta \approx 1.05$ or $\theta \approx 0.349$ as decimals or $3\theta = 60^\circ$ or $\theta = 20^\circ$ as degrees for this mark). Do not allow $\tan 3\theta = \sqrt{3}$ nor $\tan^{-1} \sqrt{3} = 3\theta$. M1: Adding $\pi$ or $2\pi$ to a previous value however obtained. It is not dependent on the previous mark. (May be implied by final answer of $\frac{4\pi}{9}$ or $\frac{7\pi}{9}$). This mark may also be given for answers as decimals [4.19 or 7.33], or degrees (240 or 420). A1: Need all three correct answers in terms of $\pi$ and no extras in range. NB: $20^\circ, 80^\circ, 140^\circ$ earns M1M1A0 and $0.349, 1.40$ and $2.44$ earns M1M1A0

subparts.label: (a)

markAllocation.criterion: Attempts to solve $4 \cos^2 x - \cos x - k = 0$, to give $\cos x = \dots$

subparts.guidance: M1: Applies $\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x$ (allow even if brackets are missing e.g. $4 \times 1 - \cos^2 x$). This must be awarded in (ii) (a) for an expression with k not after k = 3 is substituted. dM1: Uses formula or completion of square to obtain $\cos x = $ expression in k (Factorisation attempt is M0) A1: cao - award for their final simplified expression

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.answer: $x = 0$ and $139^\circ$ and $221^\circ$ (allow awrt 139 and 221) must be in degrees

markAllocation.criterion: Obtains two values for $\cos x$ from $k=3$

subparts.guidance: M1: Either attempts to substitute k = 3 into their answer to obtain two values for $\cos x$ Or restarts with k = 3 to find two values for $\cos x$ (They cannot earn marks in ii(a) for this). In both cases they need to have applied $\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x$ (brackets may be missing) and correct method for solving their quadratic (usual rules – see notes) The values for $\cos x$ may be >1 or < -1. dM1: Obtains two correct values for x A1: Obtains all three correct values in degrees (allow awrt 139 and 221) including 0. Ignore excess answers outside range (including 360 degrees) Lose this mark for excess answers in the range or radian answers.
